title: Confusion Matrix and Classification Metrics
concept:
For binary classification, a **confusion matrix** organises predictions into four outcomes:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP (True Positive) | FN (False Negative / Type II) |
| **Actual Negative** | FP (False Positive / Type I) | TN (True Negative) |

**Key metrics derived from the matrix:**

- **Accuracy** = (TP + TN) / (TP + TN + FP + FN) — overall proportion correct. Misleading with imbalanced classes.
- **Precision** = TP / (TP + FP) — "Of all items predicted positive, what fraction truly are positive?" High precision = few false alarms.
- **Recall (Sensitivity)** = TP / (TP + FN) — "Of all actual positives, what fraction did we correctly identify?" High recall = few missed positives.
- **F1-Score** = 2 × (Precision × Recall) / (Precision + Recall) — harmonic mean; balances precision and recall.

**Trade-off**: Raising the classification threshold increases precision but decreases recall (and vice versa). The right balance depends on the domain: medical screening prioritises recall (missing a disease is costly); spam filtering prioritises precision (blocking legitimate email is costly).

FP = Type I error; FN = Type II error.
keyPoints: The confusion matrix divides predictions into TP, TN, FP (Type I error), and FN (Type II error) — each has a different cost depending on the domain.
Precision measures the quality of positive predictions (TP/(TP+FP)); Recall measures coverage of actual positives (TP/(TP+FN)); F1 harmonically balances both.
Accuracy alone is misleading for imbalanced datasets — a model predicting all negatives can have high accuracy but zero recall.

quiz.question: Given TP=78, TN=100, FP=22, FN=20, what is the Recall (to 2 decimal places)?
quiz.explanation: Recall = TP / (TP + FN) = 78 / (78 + 20) = 78 / 98 ≈ 0.796, which rounds to 0.80. Precision would be 78/(78+22) = 0.78, and Accuracy would be (78+100)/(78+100+22+20) = 178/220 ≈ 0.81.
